title: Chapter 15: Three Months of Fire
content: The second month was when Sera stopped sleeping.

Three days without rest. Then four. Then five. Her body no longer needed it — the transformation was accelerating, remaking her metabolism into something that ran on the Pact's energy instead of food and oxygen. She ate less. Breathed less. Her heartbeat, which had always been a steady counterpoint to Kael's through the bond, began to slow into something that wasn't quite human.

"I can feel the sun," she told him one night. They were on the Keep's highest balcony, looking out at Greyhaven's rebuilt streets — new torches, new barracks, new humans walking freely where they'd once shuffled in blood-bound servitude. "Not the way you feel it. Not the warmth. The *presence*. Every morning, when it rises, I feel it through the Pact. Through you. It's like..." She searched for words. "Like hearing music from another room. You can't make out the melody, but you know it's beautiful."
"Is it hurting you?"

"No. It's the opposite of hurting." She held up her hand, and the golden veins pulsed beneath her skin like slow lightning. "I think the sun is healing me. Whatever the transformation is doing to my body, the sunlight is... guiding it. Shaping it. Making sure I don't become something broken."
"But you're still changing."

"I'm still changing." She lowered her hand. "The library texts say the transformation completes at Prince rank. When you become a Prince, I become whatever I'm supposed to be. Until then, I'm in between. Neither human nor vampire. Neither mortal nor immortal. Just..." She smiled, a pale echo of the fierce grin she'd worn in the ruined church. "Just yours."

Kael didn't know what to say to that. He'd never been good with words — his talent was tactics, not poetry. So he did what he always did when words failed him. He took her hand and stood with her in the darkness and waited for the dawn.
The third month was when Commander Voss arrived.

He came at night, because he was human and humans still traveled at night in the territory. But he came alone, which was stranger. The leader of the Silver Dawn, the most powerful human in the eastern territories, walking into a vampire city without escort, without guards, without anything but the Dawnbreaker blade at his hip and the iron-gray certainty in his eyes.
Sera saw him first. She was in the blood market, helping Marcus negotiate a new trade agreement with the human merchants, when the crowd parted and her father was standing there.
The silence between them was louder than any explosion.

"Sera." His voice was raw. He'd thought she was dead. The official report had listed her as killed in action. He'd mourned her. He'd drunk to her memory. And now she was standing in front of him with golden veins and luminous eyes and the unmistakable aura of someone who'd been changed by forces no human understood.
"Father." Her voice was steadier than his. "I can explain."

"Can you?" He took a step forward. The crowd around them had gone absolutely still. "Can you explain why you're alive when I spent three months thinking you were dead? Can you explain why you're glowing like a lantern? Can you explain why my daughter — my *human* daughter — is standing in a vampire city looking like she's halfway to becoming one of them?"
"The Blood Pact saved my life. I was dying. Venom in my heart. Kael found me in a church and we—"

"Kael Ashworth. The daylight Viscount." Voss's hand went to his sword. "I've read the reports. A fledgling who was Silver Dawn. Third Platoon scout. Turned during the eastern assault and abandoned." His eyes moved to Kael, who had appeared at Sera's side during the exchange. "You turned my daughter."

"I saved your daughter. The Pact was her idea. She was dying, Commander. Fifteen minutes from the venom reaching her heart. She offered me the bond because it was the only way either of us survived." Kael met Voss's eyes without flinching. "And it worked. She's alive. She's stronger than she's ever been. And she's still your daughter."

"Is she?" Voss looked at Sera again — at the golden veins, the luminous eyes, the faint glow that surrounded her like a halo. "Is she still human? Still the girl I raised? Still the paladin who told me she'd rather die than become one of the monsters?"

"I'm not a monster, Father." Sera's voice cracked but didn't break. "I'm not human anymore, either. I'm something new. Something that doesn't fit in any of your categories." She stepped toward him, and the crowd parted further. "But I'm still me. I still remember everything. The training. The missions. The fights we had when I told you I was joining the paladins instead of the tactician corps. The way you looked at me the morning I left — proud and terrified and trying not to show either one."
Voss's hand stayed on his sword. But he didn't draw it.
"Why are you here?" Sera asked. "You didn't come just to yell at me."

"I came because the eastern Counts are mobilizing. Orsi's coalition. They've got support from at least one Duke and they're massing at our border. My scouts say they're going to hit Greyhaven within the month." Voss's jaw tightened. "And I came because I need allies. Even if those allies are vampires."
The meeting in the war room lasted until dawn.

Voss laid out his intelligence — troop movements, supply lines, the names of the Counts who'd thrown in with Orsi. Kael countered with his own information — the Keep's defenses, the Polaris Guard's numbers, the strategic value of an army that could fight at noon.
By the time the sun rose over Greyhaven, they had the outline of an alliance.

"Understand something," Voss said as he prepared to leave. "This isn't forgiveness. This isn't trust. You're a vampire, and you've dragged my daughter into something I don't understand. But you're also the only vampire who's ever freed humans instead of enslaving them. And you're the only one who might be able to stop Orsi before he rolls over the entire eastern territory."
"I understand," Kael said.

"Good." Voss looked at Sera one last time. "When this is over — when Orsi is dead and the eastern territories are secure — we're going to have a longer conversation. About what you've become. About what the Pact is doing to you. About whether there's a way to reverse it."
"There isn't," Sera said quietly. "The transformation is permanent. When Kael becomes a Prince, I become whatever I'm becoming. There's no going back."
Voss's expression didn't change. But something in his eyes — iron-gray, same as his daughter's — flickered.
"Then I hope he becomes a Prince soon," he said. "Because I'd like to meet whoever you're turning into."
He left.
And somewhere in the eastern territories, Count Orsi's army began to march.
